title: SEGMENT 16 — Antibiotic stewardship: the policy and practice fix

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — Antibiotic stewardship: the policy and practice fix
You cannot drug your way out of antibiotic resistance.

Even with the best pipeline imaginable, deploying a new antibiotic the way we deployed the old ones — broadly, often unnecessarily, at inadequate doses, in agriculture as much as in medicine — would result in resistance to the new drug on the same timeline.
The fix is not just new drugs.
The fix is using the drugs we have, and the drugs we will have, much more carefully.
That is what antibiotic stewardship means.
Stewardship operates at multiple levels.

At the prescriber level: prescribe antibiotics only when bacterial infection is likely (most acute respiratory infections are viral and should not get antibiotics); choose the narrowest-spectrum drug that covers the likely pathogen rather than reflexively reaching for a broad-spectrum agent; use the shortest effective course; de-escalate from empiric broad coverage to targeted therapy once culture results are back.
A surprising amount of antibiotic use in outpatient practice is for viral upper respiratory infections, where antibiotics provide no benefit and create selection pressure.

At the hospital level: formal antibiotic stewardship programs with infectious-disease physicians and pharmacists reviewing prescribing patterns, restricting certain drugs to require approval, providing prescribing decision support, and feeding back data to clinicians about their own prescribing behaviors.

The CDC's "Core Elements of Hospital Antibiotic Stewardship Programs" — leadership commitment, accountability, drug expertise, action, tracking, reporting, education — has become the standard framework.

At the national level: surveillance networks tracking resistance patterns; national stewardship guidelines; restrictions on agricultural use; subscription-based pricing models to make novel antibiotic development economically viable; investment in diagnostic technology to allow rapid identification of pathogens and resistance.

At the global level: WHO's Global Action Plan on Antimicrobial Resistance; international surveillance through GLASS (Global Antimicrobial Resistance Surveillance System); pressure on countries with high consumption rates and lax regulation; coordinated action on agricultural use.
The evidence that stewardship works is unambiguous.
Hospitals with active stewardship programs see reductions in antibiotic use, reductions in C. difficile infection, and reductions in resistance prevalence over time, with no harm to patient outcomes.

The Dutch and Scandinavian healthcare systems, which have had aggressive stewardship and infection-control programs for decades, have MRSA rates an order of magnitude lower than the US or Southern Europe.
Stewardship is not a panacea, but it is the most tractable and well-evidenced intervention we have.
The hard part is sustaining it.
Stewardship requires resources, attention, and a willingness to occasionally tell colleagues that their preferred prescribing pattern is wrong.
It is institutionally easier to write the prescription than to refuse it.
The fight has to be fought day by day, in every hospital, indefinitely.
